agent_output:
```json
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "A logistics platform introducing automated route optimization for drivers at shift start, with dynamic recalculation triggers, customer notification windows, vehicle class constraints, and configurable stop duration estimates. This feature spans multiple bounded contexts: route planning, delivery lifecycle, fleet/driver management, and customer communication.",
    "bounded_context": "Multiple bounded contexts identified: (1) Route Planning — owns route calculation, optimization, recalculation triggers, stop ordering, and ETAs; (2) Delivery Management — owns delivery lifecycle (pending, in-progress, failed, completed), zone assignment, and delivery metadata; (3) Fleet Management — owns driver profiles, shift lifecycle, vehicle class licensing, and zone assignments; (4) Customer Notification — owns notification triggers, delivery windows, and customer-facing communication.",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Shift", "definition": "A bounded work period during which a driver is available for deliveries. A shift has a start event that triggers initial route assignment." },
      { "term": "Optimized Route", "definition": "An ordered sequence of delivery stops calculated to minimize total travel time/distance for a driver within their assigned zone, respecting vehicle class constraints." },
      { "term": "Zone", "definition": "A geographic area within which deliveries are grouped and assigned to drivers. Zones may have zone-specific operational overrides (e.g., stop duration)." },
      { "term": "Pending Delivery", "definition": "A delivery that has been created and is awaiting assignment to a driver's route. Only pending deliveries within the driver's zone are eligible for route inclusion." },
      { "term": "Route Recalculation", "definition": "The act of recomputing the optimized route in response to a triggering event: new delivery added to zone, delivery marked as failed, or driver deviation from suggested route." },
      { "term": "Failed Delivery", "definition": "A delivery that could not be completed at the stop (e.g., recipient unavailable). Triggers route recalculation to remove the stop and re-optimize remaining stops." },
      { "term": "Driver Deviation", "definition": "When a driver's actual position or stop sequence materially diverges from the suggested route, indicating they are no longer following the optimized path." },
      { "term": "Next 3 Stops Window", "definition": "A rolling window of the three upcoming delivery stops on the driver's current route. Entry into this window triggers customer notification." },
      { "term": "Vehicle Class", "definition": "A classification of vehicle type (e.g., motorcycle, van, truck, freight) that determines which categories of deliveries a driver is licensed and equipped to handle." },
      { "term": "Licensed Vehicle Class", "definition": "The vehicle class(es) a driver is authorized to operate during a shift, derived from their licensing and the vehicle they are operating." },
      { "term": "Stop Duration Estimate", "definition": "The estimated time a driver spends at each delivery stop. Defaults to 4 minutes but is overridable by the operations team at the zone level." },
      { "term": "Delivery-Vehicle Class Compatibility", "definition": "The mapping between a delivery's requirements (size, weight, type) and the vehicle classes capable of fulfilling it. A hard constraint that must never be violated." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "When a driver starts their shift, the system must automatically assign them an optimized route composed of all pending deliveries in their zone that are compatible with their licensed vehicle class.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Automatic assignment at shift start is a workflow policy. The business could decide to allow manual route creation or delayed assignment without corrupting system state.",
      "testable_assertion": "ASSERT: Within T seconds of a shift-start event, a route exists for that driver containing only deliveries that are (a) pending, (b) in the driver's zone, and (c) compatible with the driver's vehicle class."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "A driver must never be assigned a delivery that is incompatible with their licensed vehicle class. A motorcycle driver cannot carry freight; a van driver cannot carry oversized freight requiring a truck.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a physical and legal safety constraint. Assigning an incompatible delivery creates real-world danger (overloaded vehicle, regulatory violation). If this is violated, the system is in a corrupt state — the delivery is physically unfullfillable and potentially illegal.",
      "testable_assertion": "ASSERT: For every delivery D on driver X's route, D.requiredVehicleClass is contained in X.licensedVehicleClasses. No exceptions. No overrides."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "The route must be recalculated when a new delivery is added to the driver's zone (and is compatible with their vehicle class).",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Recalculation policy could be changed to batch recalculations or defer to driver discretion without data corruption.",
      "testable_assertion": "ASSERT: When a new pending delivery is created in zone Z, all active routes for drivers in zone Z are marked for recalculation and recalculated within T seconds."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "The route must be recalculated when a delivery on the route is marked as failed.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Recalculation trigger is a business policy. The system could instead simply skip the failed stop without full recalculation.",
      "testable_assertion": "ASSERT: When delivery D on route R is marked as failed, route R is recalculated excluding D and re-optimized for remaining stops."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "The route must be recalculated when the driver manually deviates from the suggested route.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Deviation-triggered recalculation is a policy choice. Some businesses allow drivers to proceed freestyle after deviation.",
      "testable_assertion": "ASSERT: When driver deviation is detected (driver's actual sequence or position diverges beyond threshold from planned route), a recalculation is triggered using the driver's current position as the new origin."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "Customers must be notified when their delivery enters the 'next 3 stops' window on the driver's active route.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Notification is a business policy. The window size (3) and the notification itself are configurable preferences, not data integrity constraints.",
      "testable_assertion": "ASSERT: When delivery D is at position N in the route where N <= 3 (and D's customer has not already been notified for this route instance), a notification is sent to D's customer."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "The default estimated stop duration is 4 minutes per delivery stop.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a configurable operational parameter, not a system integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "ASSERT: When no zone-level override exists, ETA calculations use 4 minutes per stop. When a zone override exists, that value is used instead."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "The operations team can override the stop duration estimate on a per-zone basis.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is an administrative policy about who can change configuration. A VP could change this to per-route or per-driver without system corruption.",
      "testable_assertion": "ASSERT: A zone-level stop duration override, when set, is used in all ETA calculations for routes in that zone, replacing the 4-minute default."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-9",
      "rule": "A route may only contain deliveries within the driver's assigned zone.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Zone assignment is a data integrity constraint that ensures geographic coherence. A route containing cross-zone deliveries would produce corrupt optimization results (nonsensical paths) and violate the zone-based operational model. If the business wants cross-zone, the zone model itself must change.",
      "testable_assertion": "ASSERT: For every delivery D on driver X's route, D.zone == X.assignedZone."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-10",
      "rule": "A recalculation must preserve the vehicle class constraint — newly added deliveries from a zone-level trigger must still be checked for vehicle class compatibility before inclusion.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a restatement of BR-2 applied during recalculation. The vehicle class invariant applies at all times, not just initial assignment. Any code path that adds deliveries to a route must enforce this.",
      "testable_assertion": "ASSERT: After any route recalculation, BR-2 still holds — no delivery on the route is incompatible with the driver's vehicle class."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A driver starts their shift in zone A with a motorcycle license, and zone A has 10 pending deliveries (7 motorcycle-compatible, 3 freight-only)",
      "when": "The shift-start event is processed",
      "then": "The system creates an optimized route containing exactly the 7 compatible deliveries, in optimized order, and does NOT include the 3 freight deliveries"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "A driver has an active route with 8 remaining stops in zone B",
      "when": "A new pending delivery is added to zone B that is compatible with the driver's vehicle class",
      "then": "The route is recalculated to include the new delivery, re-optimized from the driver's current position, and the updated route is presented to the driver"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "A driver has an active route with 8 remaining stops",
      "when": "Stop #4 is marked as a failed delivery",
      "then": "The route is recalculated excluding the failed delivery, re-optimized for the remaining 7 stops from the driver's current position"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "A driver has an active route and is expected at stop A next but drives to stop C instead",
      "when": "The system detects the deviation",
      "then": "The route is recalculated using the driver's actual current position as the new starting point, re-optimizing the remaining undelivered stops"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "A driver has an active route with stops [A, B, C, D, E, F] remaining",
      "when": "The driver completes a stop and the route now has stops [D, E, F, G, H] remaining",
      "then": "Customers at stops D, E, and F (the next 3) have been notified. Customers at G and H have NOT been notified yet"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "A customer was already notified when they entered the next-3 window",
      "when": "A route recalculation occurs and they remain in the next-3 window",
      "then": "The customer is NOT notified again (no duplicate notifications for the same delivery)"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-7",
      "given": "Zone C has a stop duration override of 6 minutes, zone D uses the default",
      "when": "ETAs are calculated for routes in both zones",
      "then": "Zone C routes use 6 minutes per stop in ETA calculations; zone D routes use 4 minutes per stop"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-8",
      "given": "A new delivery is added to zone B but it requires a freight vehicle",
      "when": "Route recalculation runs for a motorcycle driver in zone B",
      "then": "The freight delivery is NOT added to the motorcycle driver's route, even though it is in their zone"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-9",
      "given": "A driver has an active route and a recalculation is triggered",
      "when": "The recalculation completes and the next-3 window changes",
      "then": "Any NEW customers entering the next-3 window are notified; customers who left the window (pushed beyond position 3) are not sent a retraction"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-10",
      "given": "A driver with no pending deliveries in their zone starts their shift",
      "when": "The shift-start event is processed",
      "then": "The driver is assigned an empty route (or no route) and is placed in an 'awaiting deliveries' state — the system does not error"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Multiple recalculation triggers fire simultaneously (new delivery added AND a delivery fails at the same moment)", "expected_behavior": "Recalculations must be serialized or idempotent per route. The system should coalesce concurrent triggers into a single recalculation that accounts for all changes, not produce two conflicting route versions." },
    { "scenario": "A route recalculation moves a customer OUT of the next-3 window after they were already notified", "expected_behavior": "The notification is not retracted. The customer was told 'your delivery is coming soon' — retracting creates confusion. The delivery still happens, just later. No corrective notification unless business explicitly requires one." },
    { "scenario": "Driver's shift ends while deliveries remain on their route", "expected_behavior": "Spec does not address this. Remaining deliveries must return to 'pending' status and become eligible for reassignment. Route is deactivated. This is a critical gap in the spec." },
    { "scenario": "All deliveries in a zone fail, leaving the driver with an empty route mid-shift", "expected_behavior": "Driver enters 'awaiting deliveries' state. Route becomes empty but driver remains active and eligible for new deliveries added to the zone." },
    { "scenario": "A delivery is reassigned from one zone to another while it is on a driver's active route", "expected_behavior": "The delivery must be removed from the current route (triggering recalculation) and become pending in the new zone. The zone invariant (BR-9) must hold at all times." },
    { "scenario": "Two drivers in the same zone start their shifts simultaneously", "expected_behavior": "Deliveries must be partitioned between drivers — a single delivery cannot appear on two routes. The spec does not define the partitioning strategy (geographic sub-zones, load balancing, etc.). This is a critical gap." },
    { "scenario": "Driver deviation is detected but the driver is actually returning to the suggested route (temporary detour for fuel/restroom)", "expected_behavior": "The deviation threshold must account for minor/temporary deviations. Recalculating on every GPS jitter would be wasteful and confusing. A deviation threshold (distance or time) must be defined." },
    { "scenario": "A new delivery is added to a zone but no drivers are currently active in that zone", "expected_behavior": "The delivery remains in 'pending' status. When a driver next starts a shift in that zone, it is included in their initial route optimization." },
    { "scenario": "Customer notification fails (bad contact info, service outage)", "expected_behavior": "The delivery proceeds regardless. Notification failure must not block route progression. Failed notifications should be logged and retried per the notification system's policy." },
    { "scenario": "The driver completes deliveries faster than the stop duration estimate", "expected_behavior": "ETAs for downstream customers should be recalculated based on actual progress, not just the estimate. Otherwise, notifications become inaccurate. The spec does not address real-time ETA adjustment." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Route optimization directly reduces fuel costs, increases deliveries per shift (driver utilization), and reduces overtime. Poor optimization or vehicle class violations could result in regulatory fines, failed deliveries (re-delivery cost), and insurance liability for unsafe loading.",
    "operational": "Automated routing removes manual dispatch overhead. However, frequent recalculations under high delivery volume could create computational load. The operations team's ability to override stop duration per zone provides necessary flexibility for zones with known access difficulties (apartments, industrial parks). The feature introduces tight coupling between delivery lifecycle events and route calculation — operational monitoring of recalculation latency becomes critical.",
    "user": "Customers gain transparency via the next-3-stops notification, reducing 'where is my package' inquiries. Drivers gain efficiency from optimized routes but lose some autonomy — deviation detection may feel surveillance-like if not communicated well. Over-notification (or inaccurate ETAs from stale stop duration estimates) could erode customer trust.",
    "risk": "Primary risks: (1) Vehicle class violations are a safety and legal liability — this invariant must be bulletproof; (2) Recalculation storms under high delivery volume could degrade system performance; (3) The spec has critical gaps around multi-driver zone partitioning and shift-end handling that could cause delivery duplication or abandonment if not addressed before implementation.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Drivers", "impact": "Workflow changes significantly — routes are auto-assigned, deviations are detected. Must be communicated as efficiency aid, not surveillance. Drivers lose ability to self-organize delivery order." },
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Improved delivery visibility via next-3-stops notifications. Risk of notification fatigue or inaccurate ETAs if recalculations are frequent." },
      { "group": "Operations Team", "impact": "Gains zone-level stop duration configuration. Needs monitoring dashboards for recalculation frequency, route efficiency metrics, and vehicle class constraint enforcement." },
      { "group": "Dispatch / Planning", "impact": "Manual route planning is eliminated for covered zones. Role shifts toward exception handling and zone configuration management." },
      { "group": "Compliance / Legal", "impact": "Vehicle class enforcement is a regulatory requirement. Must be auditable — every route assignment must log the vehicle class check." },
      { "group": "Engineering / Platform", "impact": "Must handle recalculation concurrency, deviation detection thresholds, and notification deduplication. Real-time GPS integration required for deviation detection." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 55,
    "reasoning": "The spec clearly defines the core happy path and several recalculation triggers, and the vehicle class constraint is unambiguous. However, there are multiple critical gaps: (1) no definition of how deliveries are partitioned among multiple drivers in the same zone, (2) no shift-end handling, (3) no deviation threshold defined, (4) no mention of real-time ETA updates vs static estimates, (5) no concurrency strategy for simultaneous recalculation triggers. These gaps could lead to delivery duplication, abandonment, or system instability if interpreted differently by implementers.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Vehicle class constraint is a hard invariant — spec is explicit and unambiguous",
      "Recalculation triggers are clearly enumerated (new delivery, failed delivery, deviation)",
      "Customer notification window (next 3 stops) is clearly defined",
      "Stop duration default (4 min) and zone-level override authority are clear"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Multi-driver delivery partitioning within a zone — no strategy specified",
      "Shift-end behavior for undelivered stops — not addressed",
      "Deviation detection threshold — what constitutes a 'deviation' is undefined",
      "Recalculation concurrency and coalescing — no strategy specified",
      "Whether ETAs update in real-time based on actual driver progress or only at recalculation",
      "Whether the next-3-stops notification window size (3) is configurable or hardcoded"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "A driver is assigned to exactly one zone per shift",
      "A delivery belongs to exactly one zone at any given time",
      "Vehicle class compatibility is a many-to-many relationship (a delivery may be fulfillable by multiple vehicle classes, a driver may hold multiple class licenses)",
      "The 'next 3 stops' window is evaluated after every route change and after every stop completion",
      "Notifications are idempotent per customer per delivery — a customer is notified at most once per delivery entering the window",
      "Route optimization considers travel distance/time but the specific algorithm is an implementation detail, not a domain concern"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Multi-driver zone partitioning: When two or more drivers are active in the same zone, how are pending deliveries divided among them? Options include geographic sub-zoning, load balancing by count, load balancing by estimated time, or nearest-driver assignment. This fundamentally affects route quality and fairness.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Customers", "Operations Team"],
      "options": [
        "Nearest-driver assignment: each delivery goes to the driver currently closest to it",
        "Load-balanced partitioning: deliveries are split to equalize estimated shift duration across drivers",
        "Sub-zone assignment: zone is subdivided and each driver owns a sub-zone",
        "Capacity-based: assign based on remaining vehicle capacity"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Nearest-driver with load-balancing constraint (no driver gets more than 120% of average load) — balances efficiency with fairness. But this needs product confirmation."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "Shift-end handling: The spec does not define what happens to deliveries remaining on a driver's route when their shift ends. This is critical — without a defined behavior, deliveries could be silently abandoned.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Drivers", "Operations Team"],
      "options": [
        "Remaining deliveries return to pending pool and are reassigned to the next available driver",
        "Driver is prompted to extend shift to complete remaining deliveries",
        "Operations is alerted and manually reassigns"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Remaining deliveries return to pending pool automatically, with an operations alert if the count exceeds a threshold. Driver should not be forced to extend."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Deviation detection threshold: What constitutes a 'manual deviation'? A wrong turn corrected in 30 seconds should not trigger recalculation. A driver skipping 3 stops to go to a preferred one should. The spec provides no threshold definition.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Engineering / Platform"],
      "options": [
        "Distance-based: recalculate if driver is >500m from expected route for >2 minutes",
        "Sequence-based: recalculate if driver arrives at a stop that is not the next expected stop",
        "Hybrid: both distance and sequence deviation detected"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Sequence-based detection (driver arrives at a stop out of order) is simplest, most reliable, and avoids GPS noise issues. Distance-based can be added later as an enhancement."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "Recalculation concurrency: Under high delivery volume, multiple triggers could fire in rapid succession. Without a coalescing or debouncing strategy, the system could enter a recalculation storm, degrading performance and producing route flicker for the driver.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Engineering / Platform"],
      "options": [
        "Debounce: wait N seconds after the first trigger before recalculating, collecting all changes",
        "Queue with coalescing: enqueue recalculation requests and process only the latest state",
        "Lock-based: one recalculation at a time per route, subsequent triggers re-enqueue"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Debounce with a 10-15 second window — collects all near-simultaneous triggers into a single recalculation. Simple and effective."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
```
